steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The goal is to make a generalization about minerals using the notes. A generalization states a rule that applies broadly, not just a specific instance. The notes supply that rule: any mineral with a larger Mohs number can scratch any mineral with a smaller Mohs number.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Based on their Mohs scale numbers, quartz (7) is harder than apatite (5), and apatite is harder than calcite (3).': Lists specific rankings for these three minerals but never states the underlying principle that explains why. It is an enumeration, not a generalization. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Any mineral with a Mohs number of 7, like quartz, can scratch a mineral with a Mohs number of 5, like apatite.': The word "any" signals a general rule rather than a specific observation. It states the principle from the notes (higher number scratches lower number) and illustrates it with examples. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Quartz can leave visible scratches on calcite, which is why quartz has a higher number than calcite on the Mohs scale of mineral hardness.': Reverses the causal direction in the notes. The notes say the higher number reflects greater hardness; they do not say scratching causes a mineral to receive a higher number. ✗
  • Choice D — 'The Mohs scale can be used to order calcite, quartz, and apatite by their ability to scratch other minerals.': Describes what the scale does in general but makes no claim about the specific relationship among these minerals and states no generalizable rule about hardness. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
